title: On
description: The #[On] attribute allows a component to listen for events and execute a method when those events are dispatched.

sections.title: #Basic usage
sections.content:
Apply the #[On] attribute to any method that should be called when an event is dispatched:
When another component dispatches the post-created event, the updatePostList() method will be called automatically.

examples.code: <?php // resources/views/components/⚡dashboard.blade.php use Livewire\Attributes\On;use Livewire\Component; new class extends Component { #[On('post-created')] public function updatePostList($title) { session()->flash('status', "New post created: {$title}"); }};

sections.title: #Dispatching events
sections.content:
To dispatch an event that triggers listeners, use the dispatch() method:
The post-created event will trigger any methods decorated with #[On('post-created')].

examples.code: <?php // resources/views/components/post/⚡create.blade.php use Livewire\Component;use App\Models\Post; new class extends Component { public $title = '';  public function save() { $post = Post::create(['title' => $this->title]);  $this->dispatch('post-created', title: $post->title);   return redirect('/posts'); }};

sections.title: #Passing data to listeners
sections.content: Events can pass data as named parameters:

examples.code: // Dispatching with multiple parameters$this->dispatch('post-updated', id: $post->id, title: $post->title);
// Listening and receiving parameters#[On('post-updated')]public function handlePostUpdate($id, $title){ // Use $id and $title...}

sections.title: #Dynamic event names
sections.content:
You can use component properties in event names for scoped listening:
If $post->id is 3, this will only listen for post-updated.3 events, ignoring updates to other posts.

examples.code: <?php // resources/views/components/post/⚡show.blade.php use Livewire\Attributes\On;use Livewire\Component;use App\Models\Post; new class extends Component { public Post $post;  #[On('post-updated.{post.id}')] public function refreshPost() { $this->post->refresh(); }};

sections.title: #Multiple event listeners
sections.content: A single method can listen for multiple events:

examples.code: #[On('post-created')]#[On('post-updated')]#[On('post-deleted')]public function refreshStats(){ // Refresh statistics when any post changes}

sections.title: #Listening to browser events
sections.content: You can also listen for browser events dispatched from JavaScript:

sections.title: #Alternative: Listening in the template
sections.content:
Instead of using the attribute, you can listen for events directly on child components in your Blade template:
This listens for the saved event from the post.edit child component and refreshes the parent when it's dispatched.
You can also call specific methods:

sections.title: #Example: Real-time notifications
sections.content:
Here's a practical example of a notification bell that listens for new notifications:
Other components can dispatch events to update the notification count:

examples.code: unreadCount = auth()->user()->unreadNotifications()->count(); }  #[On('notification-sent')] public function incrementCount() { $this->unreadCount++; }  #[On('notifications-read')] public function resetCount() { $this->unreadCount = 0; }};?>

sections.title: #Learn more
sections.content: For more information about events, dispatching to specific components, and Laravel Echo integration, see the Events documentation.
